title: IPOs — How Companies Go Public
objectives: Explain the IPO process step by step
Describe why some IPOs succeed and others fail
Evaluate whether buying IPO shares is a good strategy for ordinary investors

vocabulary.definition: When a private company sells shares to the public for the first time, listing on a stock exchange and raising capital.
A legal document published before an IPO describing the company's business, financials, risks and use of the money raised. Investors must read this before investing.
Usually an investment bank that guarantees the IPO will raise its target amount, buying any unsold shares itself. Takes a fee of 2% to 7% of the amount raised.
When demand for IPO shares exceeds supply. If an IPO is 3× oversubscribed, investors applied for 3× as many shares as available. Often leads to share price rising on listing day.
The quick profit made by buying IPO shares and selling on the first day of trading when oversubscribed IPOs often jump in price.

activities.title: How an IPO Works
activities.notePrompt: Write the five steps of the IPO process in order. What does the prospectus tell you that you could not find anywhere else?
activities.content:
When a private company decides to 'go public,' it follows a structured process.

Step 1 — Decision and preparation (6–18 months): The company hires investment banks, auditors and lawyers. Financial records are cleaned up and audited. Management prepares to face public scrutiny.

Step 2 — Prospectus: A detailed legal document is prepared describing the business model, financial history, future plans, risks, management team, and how the money raised will be used. In NZ this is registered with the Financial Markets Authority (FMA).

Step 3 — Price setting: The offer price is set based on company valuation. The underwriter conducts a 'bookbuild' — testing demand from institutional investors to find the price the market will support.

Step 4 — Public offer: Ordinary investors can apply for shares at the IPO price during the offer period (typically 2–4 weeks). Applications are submitted through NZX-registered brokers or the company's online offer platform.

Step 5 — Listing: Shares are allocated to applicants (scaled back if oversubscribed). The company lists on the exchange and trading begins. The share price immediately finds its market level — which may be above or below the IPO price.

activities.title: Famous NZ IPOs
activities.notePrompt: For each NZ IPO example, write: Was it oversubscribed or undersubscribed? Did the share price rise or fall on listing? What does this tell you about predicting IPO performance?
activities.content:
NZ has had some memorable IPOs in recent years.

Mercury Energy (2013): The government sold part of its stake in Mercury (then Mighty River Power) at $2.50 per share as part of the Mixed Ownership Model programme. Strong retail demand. Closed above IPO price on listing day and has been a strong long-term performer.

Solomon's Gold (fictional): Illustrates the risk — a small mining company lists at $0.50 per share on excitement about gold deposits. IPO is 4× oversubscribed. Shares list at $0.90. Within 12 months the gold deposits prove smaller than expected and shares fall to $0.15.

Burger King NZ (Restaurant Brands, private placement): Not a public IPO but illustrates how valuations work — Restaurant Brands NZ was taken private in 2023 after trading publicly for many years.

The lesson: IPOs are exciting but unpredictable. Oversubscribed IPOs often jump on listing day (tempting stag profits) but the long-term performance depends entirely on the underlying business. Research consistently shows IPOs underperform the broader market on average over 3-5 years — because companies tend to list when market conditions favour the seller, not the buyer.

activities.statement: Because an IPO is oversubscribed (too many buyers), the share price almost always continues rising for years after listing.
activities.explanation: False. Oversubscription predicts short-term listing day performance reasonably well — strong demand often pushes prices up on day one. But long-term performance has no reliable correlation with IPO oversubscription. Many oversubscribed IPOs significantly underperform the broader market over 3–5 years. Research shows companies tend to time IPOs when market conditions and their own valuations are most favourable to them as sellers — which is not necessarily the best time to be a buyer.

activities.title: The Prospectus — What to Read Before Investing
activities.notePrompt: List five sections of an IPO prospectus you should read before applying for shares. Why is the 'risk factors' section often the most important?
activities.content:
A prospectus is a legal document that must disclose all material information about a company before its IPO. In NZ, it is registered with the FMA. False or misleading statements in a prospectus can result in criminal charges for directors.

Key sections to read:

Business description: What does the company actually do? How does it make money? Who are its customers?

Financial statements: Revenue, profit, debt, cash flow for the past 3 years. Is the company growing? Is it profitable? How much debt does it carry?

Use of proceeds: What will the company do with the money raised? Expansion (positive) or primarily paying off existing investor loans (less positive for new investors)?

Risk factors: Every material risk the company faces — competition, regulation, key person dependency, economic sensitivity. Companies are legally required to be honest here. Read this section carefully.

Management team: Who runs the company? Do they have relevant experience? Do they have skin in the game (holding significant shares post-IPO)?

Valuation rationale: At what P/E ratio or other multiple is the company being priced? Is this reasonable compared to listed peers?

A company that spends 3 pages on risk factors is being honest. One that has half a page on risks for a complex business is a warning sign.

questions.question: A NZ tech company IPO is 5× oversubscribed — investors applied for 5 times the available shares. You applied for 1,000 shares. Approximately how many shares are you likely to receive?
options: 5,000 shares — oversubscription means you receive more than you applied for as compensation
1,000 shares — your application is guaranteed at IPO price if you apply on time
Approximately 200 shares — if scaled back proportionally based on 5× oversubscription
No shares — oversubscribed IPOs only allocate shares to institutional investors
questions.explanation: In a 5× oversubscribed IPO, allocations are typically scaled back proportionally. If you applied for 1,000 shares at 5× oversubscription, you receive approximately 1,000 ÷ 5 = 200 shares (though the exact allocation method varies — some IPOs prioritise smaller retail applications, some scale back all applications equally).

questions.question: A mining company's IPO prospectus has 2 pages covering company history and vision, and 18 pages of detailed risk factors. What should this suggest to an investor?
options: The company is poorly managed — a well-run company would have fewer risks to disclose
The company's lawyers are unnecessarily cautious and risk-averse
The company is being relatively honest and thorough about its risks — extensive risk disclosure is a sign of good faith and regulatory compliance, not necessarily a sign to avoid
The IPO will definitely underperform because so many risks are listed
questions.explanation: Extensive risk disclosure is generally a positive sign — it means management and lawyers are being honest about what could go wrong. Minimal risk factors in a complex business is the actual warning sign. Reading the risk factors carefully lets you decide whether you are comfortable with the specific risks. No investment is risk-free and a prospectus that pretends otherwise is misleading.

activities.title: IPO Process — Put It in the Right Order

pairs.item: Company hires investment banks and auditors — preparation begins
Prospectus written and registered with the FMA
Bookbuild with institutional investors to set the offer price
Public offer period — retail investors apply for shares
Shares allocated and company begins trading on the NZX

activities.explanation: The IPO process typically takes 6 to 18 months from decision to listing. The prospectus is the critical document — it must be honest and complete. The bookbuild sets the price based on institutional demand. Once trading begins, the market takes over and prices reflect continuous buyer-seller interactions.

activities.statement: Research shows IPO shares consistently outperform the broader market index over a 3 to 5 year period after listing.
activities.explanation: False. Academic research consistently shows that on average, IPOs underperform the broader market index over 3 to 5 years. The reason: companies tend to time their IPOs when their business and market conditions are most favourable to them as sellers — meaning the public is often buying at a peak of optimism. The exception is oversubscribed IPOs of genuinely exceptional businesses, but identifying these in advance is difficult.

activities.question: What is the legal document published before an IPO that must disclose the company's business, financials, risks and use of money raised?
activities.hint: It starts with P and is a legal requirement for NZ IPOs.
activities.explanation: The prospectus is the foundational document for any IPO. In NZ it must be registered with the Financial Markets Authority (FMA). Directors face legal liability for false or misleading statements in a prospectus — which is why the risk factors section tends to be thorough. Reading the prospectus is the single most important thing an investor can do before participating in an IPO.

activities.title: Would You Participate in an IPO?
activities.prompt: Imagine a popular NZ technology company is about to IPO. The company makes software used by 60% of NZ businesses. The IPO is 4× oversubscribed. The offer price implies a P/E ratio of 45× earnings. Based on what you have learned, would you apply for shares? What information from the prospectus would you check first? What would make you say no?
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — critical thinking about IPOs is one of the most valuable skills for any investor.
